coredata.dc:title: Chitosan/gelatin-based multifunctional film integrated with green tea carbon dots to extend the shelf life of pork
coredata.dc:creator: Khan, Ajahar
Ezati, Parya
Rhim, Jong-Whan
coredata.dc:description: A multifunctional packaging film for extending the shelf life of minced pork was developed by integrating green tea-derived carbon dots (GT-CD) into a chitosan/gelatin (Ch/Gel) polymer blend. The effects of different amounts of GT-CD on the films' antibacterial, antioxidant, and other physicochemical characteristics were investigated. The Ch/Gel film with 3 wt% GT-CD showed high antioxidant activity (99.2% for ABTS and 63.87% for DPPH) and strong antibacterial action (100% eradicating E. coli and L. monocytogenes within 3 h) without significant changes in their film characteristic such as the water vapor permeability. In addition, the prepared film showed excellent UV-blocking properties (∼90.23% of UV-A, 99.65% of UV-B). When packaged with the functional film, the pork showed reduced bacterial growth (less than 1 Log CFU/g after 24 h) and preserved the meat color even when stored at 20 °C for 24 h. The GT-CD-added Ch/Gel-based films with excellent functional properties such as antioxidant, antimicrobial, and UV-barrier properties have a strong probability for active food packaging, particularly for extending shelf life and preserving the visual quality of packaged meat.

head.ce:title: Chitosan/gelatin-based multifunctional film integrated with green tea carbon dots to extend the shelf life of pork

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation and analysis, Writing − original draft, Validation
Methodology, Investigation, Data curation and analysis, Writing − original draft, Validation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A multifunctional packaging film for extending the shelf life of minced pork was developed by integrating green tea-derived carbon dots (GT-CD) into a chitosan/gelatin (Ch/Gel) polymer blend. The effects of different amounts of GT-CD on the films' antibacterial, antioxidant, and other physicochemical characteristics were investigated. The Ch/Gel film with 3 wt% GT-CD showed high antioxidant activity (99.2% for ABTS and 63.87% for DPPH) and strong antibacterial action (100% eradicating E. coli and L. monocytogenes within 3 h) without significant changes in their film characteristic such as the water vapor permeability. In addition, the prepared film showed excellent UV-blocking properties (∼90.23% of UV-A, 99.65% of UV-B). When packaged with the functional film, the pork showed reduced bacterial growth (less than 1 Log CFU/g after 24 h) and preserved the meat color even when stored at 20 °C for 24 h. The GT-CD-added Ch/Gel-based films with excellent functional properties such as antioxidant, antimicrobial, and UV-barrier properties have a strong probability for active food packaging, particularly for extending shelf life and preserving the visual quality of packaged meat.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ensuring food safety is a major concern for manufacturers, monitoring agencies, and consumers. Despite advances in production technology, food contamination by foodborne pathogenic bacteria remains a global problem (Dhar et al., 2022). According to previous reports, outbreaks of foodborne pathogens cost more than $6 billion in the United States alone (Zhang et al., 2021). The meat industry is a prime example of how spoilage of meat products during processing, distribution, and supply to consumers has a strong negative effect on the meat sector (Wrona et al., 2021). Two major problems during meat product processing, storage, and distribution are degradation due to lipid oxidation and microbial growth. The deterioration of meat products causes huge economic losses to the meat business (up to 40% of production) (Lorenzo et al., 2014). Therefore, the first step is to increase our understanding of the reactions that undertake the spoilage of meat to develop and choose suitable packaging formats and materials to lessen the quality loss (Fang et al., 2017; Khan et al., 2023a, 2023b).

Further, the high trend towards adaptation of petroleum-based plastic packaging has created complications as these packaging materials can take hundreds of years to decompose, causing serious environmental pollution and health problems (Ong et al., 2022). Additionally, prevailing food packaging technology efficiently succeeds in food quality, but it is still inadequate to prevent loss because of photooxidation and contamination of microorganisms during processing, storage, and supply (Zhang et al., 2021). In this regard, active packaging containing antibacterial and antioxidant agents can protect the meat from microbial contamination and lipid oxidation (Umaraw et al., 2020). Therefore, with growing consumer concerns about food's sustainability, safety, quality, and environmental impact, designing cost-effective and environmentally safe high-quality food packaging materials, especially biodegradable and active packaging materials with antibacterial and antioxidant effects, is essential.

Bio-derived raw materials such as polysaccharides, lipids, proteins, carbohydrates, and cellulose exist in abundance on Earth. Their sustainability, renewability, biodegradability, biocompatibility, non-toxicity, and eco-friendliness make them a promising alternative to synthetic polymer packaging (Dutta & Sit, 2022). These biopolymers incorporated with suitable natural additives and nanofillers improve the packaging materials' antioxidant, antimicrobial, oxygen barrier, and mechanical properties. One of these biopolymers, gelatin, is a good choice for a biodegradable film because it is inexpensive, abundantly accessible, and has brilliant mechanical properties (Min et al., 2022). Gelatin is a protein obtained through the hydrolysis of collagen and is extensively used as a film-forming material in the food, pharmaceutical, and supplement industries. However, gelatin films are limited in their use in food packaging due to their strong hydrophilicity (Ezati et al., 2022a). A method for preparing a composite film by adding chitosan to gelatin has been proposed to overcome the limitations of gelatin film. Chitosan, like gelatin, is a bio-based polymer that is widely used and has a brilliant film-forming ability (Wang et al., 2021). In addition, mixing chitosan and gelatin can create an integrated composite network due to some interaction/association through intermolecular hydrogen bonding between polymer chains, improving the physicochemical and mechanical properties of the composite film (Wang et al., 2021). Chitosan/gelatin (Ch/Gel)-based films possessed some antioxidant and antibacterial activity.

Still, recently, the approach of manufacturing a composite film by encapsulating functional nanomaterials has been widely employed to improve the functional parameters of the film. Recently, interest in using carbon dots (CD) as a functional filler for biopolymeric films has been increasing (Khan et al., 2023a, 2023c; Zhang et al., 2017). CD (size <10 nm) have emerged as potential functional fillers for food safety purposes because of their unique features such as environmental sustainability, good chemical stability, ease of synthesis and surface functionalization, good compatibility, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and hydrophilicity (Ezati et al., 2022b; Liu et al., 2019). CDs with these properties are already extensively employed in biomedical applications, for instance, drug delivery, photodynamic therapy, bioimaging, biolabeling, and biosensing (Salimi Shahraki et al. (2022)).

CD can be manufactured using various food processing by-products or natural agricultural and fishery resources; green tea leaves are one of these natural resources. Green tea is expected to be a good raw material for the functional CD because it contains many active substances such as flavonoids, amino acids, vitamins, catechins, tea polyphenols, and caffeine. These compounds in green tea have recently attracted considerable attention due to their potential beneficial effects on human health, having anti-inflammatory, anti-platelet, anti-allergic, anti-viral, antibacterial, antioxidant, and anti-tumor activities (Lambert et al., 2010; Rietveld & Wiseman, 2003). The main compounds with antibacterial and antioxidant properties are catechins and gallic acid (Lambert et al., 2010; Lin et al., 2008; Zuo et al., 2002). Therefore, the high antioxidant potential of green tea can be used as an excellent carbon source for multifunctional CD manufacturing. These CDs can be used as functional nano-fillers that improve physical properties such as durability, gas barrier, thermal, mechanical, and stability properties of packaging films or provide functional properties such as UV barrier, antibacterial, and antioxidant properties (Ezati et al., 2022a).

The major goal of this study was to create Ch/Gel-based multifunctional films integrated with green tea-derived CDs (GT-CDs) for active packaging purposes. To this end, the impact of the CDs on the physicochemical and functional properties of the film was evaluated. The film was employed as a pork packaging material to confirm the capability to extend the shelf life of the packaged meat.

Carbon dots (CDs) were prepared with green tea (GT) using a hydrothermal process ( Fig. 1) (Min et al., 2022; Roy et al., 2021). For this, GT (1.0 g) was mixed in distilled water (50 mL) with mild stirring at room temperature (25 ± 3 °C), transferred to a Teflon-lined cylinder, sealed in a stainless-steel reactor (100 mL), and then left for 6 h at 190 °C in a muffle furnace. The reactor was cooled to 25 °C to get the yellow-brown solution mixture. The reactant solution was filtered using a 100 µm Whatman filter paper and centrifuged for 15 min at 10,000 rpm. Further, a membrane filter (25 mm diameter nylon filter, 0.22 µm pore size, Whatman International Ltd., Maidstone, UK) was used to filter the supernatant (GT-CD solution). The obtained GT-CD solution was stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C before further testing. Detailed information for GT-CD characterization is provided in Supporting Information.

Ch/Gel-based films were fabricated using a solution-casting method (Roy et al., 2021). To prepare the Ch/Gel film solution, chitosan (2%, w/v) was dissolved in an aqueous solution of acetic acid (1%, v/v) with constant mechanical agitation at 50 °C for 30 min, and gelatin (2%, w/v) was dissolved in distilled water at 80 °C for 30 min stirring. The polymer solutions were mixed at a 1:1 ratio with continuous stirring at 40 °C, and 30 wt% of glycerol (based on polymer) as a plasticizer was added. For the construction of the CD-added films, various amounts of the GT-CD (1, 2, and 3 wt% of polymers) were integrated into the above Ch/Gel film solutions with vigorous stirring at 40 °C for 2 h. The solution mixture (200 mL) was cast on a Teflon film-coated glass plate (24 cm × 30 cm) and dried at room temperature for 48 h. After that, the films were collected and stored in a humidity set at 25 °C and 50% relative humidity for at least 48 h before further testing. Depending on the GT-CD contents, the fabricated films were designated Ch/Gel, Ch/Gel-CD1%, Ch/Gel-CD2%, and Ch/Gel-CD3% films. Detailed methods for physicochemical characterizations and film properties are described in Supporting Information.

The minced pork was used in packaging tests to investigate the film's effectiveness in preserving food freshness. A 10 g meat sample was wrapped using GT-CD with Ch/Gel film (10 cm × 10 cm). Pork samples wrapped in the fabricated film were kept at 20 °C for 2 days, and the sterilization effect was investigated every 24 h. A total viable colony count technique was adapted to estimate bacterial count for the stored pork samples. To determine the bactericidal effect, the meat samples (0.1% of 45 mL peptone water) were homogenized with a stomacher (BagMixer® 400, Interscience, France) for 180 s. After that, 1 mL of serially diluted homogenous aliquots were duplicated on 3 M™ Petrifilm counting plates. After 48 h of incubation at 37 °C, colonies were counted, and the experimental outcomes were listed as Log CFU/g. Besides bacterial effects, meat color changes were also observed and measured using a protocol discussed in Supporting Information.

To determine lipid oxidation, the meat samples were wrapped in the neat Ch/Gel, Ch/Gel-CD1%, Ch/Gel-CD2%, and Ch/Gel-CD3% films and kept at 20 °C for 48 h. Using the AOCS 965.33 approved technique, the lipid peroxidation of each sample was assessed every 24 h (Priyadarshi et al., 2022). After removing the meat sample from the test film, the fat from the mechanically minced pork was removed using Soxhlet extraction with n-hexane. The solvent was removed using a rotary evaporator before collecting the extracted fat. Then, 1 mg of fat was put into a 100 mL Erlenmeyer flask with 6 mL of a solution (3:2 v/v) of acetic acid and chloroform, 1 mL of saturated potassium iodide solution, and 6 mL of distilled water. After each addition, the mixture was manually agitated to ensure equal mixing. Next, 1 mL of the 1% starch indicator was added to the mixture to make it bluish-black. The combination was then titrated with 0.001 N sodium thiosulfate until the color of the solution gets vanished. The following equation was used to calculate the peroxidation value:
(1)
Peroxide value =
S × N × 100
W
where S denotes the amount of sodium thiosulfate used throughout the titration, N denotes the sodium thiosulfate's normality, and W denotes the weight of fat used. Peroxide levels are presented in milli-equivalents per kg of fat.

The GT-CD were synthesized at high temperatures via active substances present in green tea with a polycarboxylate/polyhydroxy compound or a nitrogen-comprising compound existing as the primary carbon source (Niu et al., 2015; Verloop et al., 2016). The size distribution, stability, functional groups, chemical composition, fluorescent response, and antimicrobial activities of the developed GT-CD were determined through the TEM, zeta potential analyses, FTIR, XPS, UV–vis/fluorescent spectra, and agar well diffusion methods. The chemical composition, structural morphology, and surface functional groups of the fabricated Ch/Gel, Ch/Gel-CD1%, Ch/Gel-CD2%, and Ch/Gel-CD3% films were characterized by FE-SEM, FTIR, XPS, TGA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM images, zeta potential, and optical properties of GT-CD
ce:section.ce:para: TEM images were recorded to determine the size distribution of the synthesized CD, as shown in Fig. 2a,b. The TEM images reveal that the GT-CD were spherical and monodisperse with a size distribution (below 10 nm) in the quantum range (Roy et al., 2021). The particle diameter for the fabricated GT-CD ranges from ∼1.91–3.02 nm. In addition, the zeta potentials value showed the samples’ surface charge. Zeta potential indicates the stability of the solution; the surface charge of particles with high zeta potentials tends to repel each other and stabilize the dispersion and retain function, and the absolute value far away from zero means more stability of the solution (Ezati et al., 2022b). The zeta potential of prepared GT-CD was around − 14.6 mV (Table S1), validating stable aqueous dispersion similar to Priyadarshi et al. reported data (Priyadarshi et al., 2021). The electrostatic repulsion between the charged functional groups on the surface of the CD likely helps stabilize the GT-CD. According to the previous reports, the values close to or higher than ± 15 mV suggest good stability of the CD dispersions in an aqueous medium (Ezati et al., 2022c).

UV–vis and fluorescent spectroscopy determined the optical characteristics of the GT-CD. The 200–600 nm UV–vis absorption spectra illustrate a similar absorption pattern to the previously reported CD (Roy et al., 2021). The aqueous solution of GT-CD exhibited strong green color when irradiated with full-band UV light (right inset, Fig. 2c). This instant blue light emission is a distinguishing phenomenon of CD, where the synthesis pathway and carbon precursor are chosen to prepare CD to portray the intensity of the emitted color (Pandiyan et al., 2020). The sharp absorption peaks positioned around 295 nm assigned to n-π * transitions (C-OH or CO bonds) verified the successful synthesis of CD (Fig. 2c) (Roy et al., 2021).

Further, to determine the fluorescent characteristics of the GT CD, the fluorescent emission spectra were recorded as the excitation wavelength increased from 320 to 460 nm with an increment of 20 nm (Fig. 2c). At an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, the CD exhibited a strong fluorescent peak in the 300–650 nm range, with the maximum intensity around 442 nm (Fig. 2d). The fluorescent emission of the proposed CD displayed a typical excitation wavelength-dependent response (320–460 nm), and the emissive wavelength was red-shifted under longer excitation wavelengths (Yang et al., 2014). Therefore, increasing the excitation wavelength from 320 to 460 nm showed a clear, steady red shift in the emission peaks (Fig. 2d). The tunable fluorescent emission of the CD has been supposed to be due to various fluorescence paraments such as carbon source, synthetic pathway, different sizes, shapes, and the presence of different emissive sites and functional groups on the surfaces of the CD. However, the precise mechanism describing CD's excitation wavelength-dependent emission characteristics has not yet been fully developed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR and XPS analyses of GT-CD
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR and XPS characterizations were conducted to investigate the GT-CDs' surface functional groups, structure, and chemical composition. As observed in Fig. 3a, the peak positioned around ∼3200 and 3080 cm−1 assigned to the stretching vibrations of -OH, N-H, and C-H bonds, respectively. The adsorption peaks at ∼2926 and ∼1238 cm−1 are formed by the asymmetric and symmetric stretching of C-H bonds in methyl and methylene groups. The absorption peaks around ∼1740–1630 cm−1 are ascribed to the CO/CC bending vibrations. Further, the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the C-N bond, the stretching vibration of the O-H (phenolic hydroxyl group) bond, and the stretching vibration of the C-O-C bond can be indicated by the adsorption peaks positioned around ∼1388, ∼1280, and ∼1043 cm−1, respectively (Fu et al., 2022a,2022b). The results of the FTIR investigation confirm the presence of phenolic hydroxyl groups in GT-CDs, demonstrating the involvement of polyphenols in GT-CD production (Fu et al., 2022a,2022b).

The XPS analysis was performed to confirm the chemical composition, structure, and existence of the constitutional elements of GT-CDs. The full survey spectrum of GT-CD is mainly composed of C (67.2%), O (29.4%), and N (3.1%), indicated by the peaks positioned around 284.52, 398.97, and 530.69 eV, respectively (Fig. 3b) (Ehrat et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2015). The deconvoluted C1s spectrum of GTP-CDs (Fig. 3c) can be split into four characteristics peaks located at ∼284.7 (indicates the C-H or C-C bonds), ∼286.3 (related to the C-O/C-N bonds), ∼287.8 eV (corresponds to the O-C-O/CO of carbonyl), and 289.8 eV corresponds to the N-CO bond (Fig. 3c) (Ehrat et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2015). The high-resolution spectrum of N1s spectra gives peaks located around and attributed due to the C-N-C (399.1 eV), tertiary N bonded to carbon atoms in the form of N-(C)3 (400.2 eV), and N-H (403.0 eV) bonds (Fig. 3d) (Xia et al., 2004; Zhang et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2015). Further, three split peaks in the deconvoluted O 1 s spectrum can be illustrated in Fig. 3e, with the peak at 531.7 eV corresponding to the C-O/N-O and -COOR in anhydride and ester. The CO bond was thought to be responsible for the peak at 534.4 eV, while phenol/C-O-H groups are indicated by the peak at 533.5 eV (Li et al., 2015; Zhao et al., 2015). In general, GT-CDs have a surface that is abundant in functional groups that contain oxygen, which makes the GT-CD easily diffused in water. The results of XPS analysis demonstrate polyphenols' involvement in the formation of CDs by revealing their distinctive structure that has retained phenolic hydroxyl groups on GT-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FE-SEM of GT-CD incorporated Ch/Gel-based films
ce:section.ce:para: The surface and cross-sectional morphology of the Ch/Gel, Ch/Gel-CD1%, Ch/Gel-CD2%, and Ch/Gel-CD3% films were observed using the FE-SEM ( Fig. 4a). The surface of the Ch/Gel and Ch/Gel-CD1% films showed uniform, continuous, intact, and smooth, indicating the formation well-blended polymer matrix. Incorporating 1 wt% GT-CD (Ch/Gel-CD1%) did not compromise the surface appearance as there were no surface cracks, suggesting that GT-CD is compatible with Ch/Gel blends. On the other hand, the microstructure of the CH/Gel-GT2% film appeared to be much more curved than the control film, and although some wavy lumps were observed in the Ch/Gel-CD3% film, the CD was consistently distributed throughout the film surface. The granular surface appearance is likely due to the increased concentration of GT-CD in the Ch/Gel film. From observing the FE-SEM images, it can be deduced that the interface compatibility between GT-CDs and the Ch/Gel polymer matrix could be responsible for the uniform dispersion of GT-CDs. Cross-sectional images also showed intact morphologies without ruptures, pores, or cavities, indicating good compatibility with GT-CD and Ch/Gel composite. The EDX spectrum and elemental mapping images were recorded to determine the main constitutional elements of the Ch/Gel-CD3% film (Fig. 4b). The EDX analysis revealed that carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen were the main elements forming the structural backbone of the Ch/Gel-CD3%. Further, it can be observed from the mapping images that the dispersion of individual elements was uniform over the surface, indicating an unvarying distribution of the GT-CD in the binary polymer film matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR of GT-CD incorporated Ch/Gel-based films
ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectra of the Ch/Gel, Ch/Gel-CD1%, Ch/Gel-CD2%, and Ch/Gel-CD3% films are displayed in Fig. 4c. A broad absorption band centered around 3200–3500 cm−1 corresponds to the overlapping of hydroxyl and amino group stretching vibration in all four films. For the FTIR spectra of Ch/Gel, Ch/Gel-CD1%, Ch/Gel-CD2%, and Ch/Gel-CD3% films, the peaks around 2930 and 2867 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of -C-H or -CH2 groups. In all the spectra, the characteristic absorption peaks around 1635, 1551, and 1237 cm−1 were due to the amide I, II, and III of gelatin and chitosan carbon chains, respectively (Kalaycıoğlu et al., 2017; Qi et al., 2017). The peaks around 1411 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of the C-N, and the bands around 1155 and 1032 cm−1 correspond to the CO stretching vibrations and saccharide structure (Roy et al., 2020). After the addition of GT-CDs, we noticed that the Ch/Gel-CD1%, Ch/Gel-CD2%, and Ch/Gel-CD3% films spectra showed the characteristic peaks of Ch and Gel in proportion to the ratio of the two components, with a slight difference in the intensity of peaks related to amino and carbonyl moieties in the 1500–1700 cm−1 range. This indicated that these groups mainly interact through electrostatic interactions (Pereda et al., 2011). Further, The broadening of the peak around 3200–3500 cm−1 and the slight shifts in the peak position of the participating groups in FTIR spectra of Ch/Gel incorporated with CD films in comparison to that of neat-Ch/Gel film infers that intermolecular hydrogen bonding between OH groups of Ch, Gel, and CD also plays a role in Ch/Gel-CD complex formation (Chen et al., 2021; Konwar et al., 2015). In all films overall, the FTIR pattern was analogous to that of the neat Ch/Gel film and had similar spectra with their characteristic peaks except for a slight change in intensity and position of peaks caused by the incorporation of GT-CD, which confirms the successful incorporation of CD in the Ch/Gel film.

The negligible change in the IR spectra after incorporating GT-CD was due to the overlapping C-H, OH, CC, and CO stretching vibration in almost the same IR region. Therefore, it can be suggested that the intensity of obtained peaks in the spectra of the CD-added films remain intact with slight shifting in the frequency regions confirming the uniform incorporation and decent compatibility of the GT-CDs with the Ch/Gel polymer matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability of GT-CD incorporated Ch/Gel-based films
ce:section.ce:para: The thermal decomposition properties of the GT-CD loaded Ch/Gel-based films were evaluated through thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) (Fig. 4d). The TGA thermograms show that multiple weight losses were noticed for all Ch/Gel films. The GT-CD first thermal decomposition observed around 50–120 °C attributed to the evaporation of physisorbed water. The second weight loss began around 120 °C and over at 285 °C with the maximum weight loss at 262 °C, which was assigned to the low-molecular proteins degradation of Gel and glycerol. The third thermal degradation of films happened around 280–420 °C, with the maximum degradation at 350 °C, assigned to the decomposition of chitosan and high-molecular-weight gelatin (Roy & Rhim, 2022). A noticeable difference in thermal stability was noticed with the integration of GT-CD into the Ch/Gel film. The GT-CD films degrade more slowly in the first degradation step as the hydrophilic CD entrapped physisorbed water molecules. As observed, the peak temperature was slightly amplified than the neat Ch/Gel film. Similarly, the GT-CD loaded films displayed slightly lower second and third-step decomposition, i.e., more thermally stable than the control film, which is probably to the existence of hydrogen bonds between the -OH groups of CD and CH2OH groups of the Ch/Gel polymer backbone, resulting in improving the thermal properties of the fabricated films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS analysis of the GT-CD incorporated Ch/Gel-based films
ce:section.ce:para: The elemental compositions and chemical state of GT-CD incorporated Ch/Gel films were identified through XPS investigation. The XPS survey spectra in Fig. 5a,b exposes that the pure-Ch/Gel and Ch/Gel-CD3% comprise carbon (C 1 s at ∼285.8 eV), nitrogen (N 1 s ∼399.2, eV), and oxygen (O 1 s at ∼532.1 eV) core levels, which was in good agreement with the EDS spectrum (Qi et al., 2017). The deconvoluted XPS C1s spectrum of Ch/Gel film (Fig. 5c,d) can be split into three distinct components peaks positioned around 284.2, 286.1, and 287.5 eV, which were indicated to C-C/CC, CN/C-O, and CO, respectively. These peaks are mainly from the groups of Ch and Gel biopolymer blends (Qi et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2019). On the other side, the deconvoluted XPS C1s spectrum of Ch/Gel-CD3% film divided into four component peaks distributed around 283.9, 285.1, 286.0, and 287.2 eV, which correspond to C-C/CC, C-N/C-H, C-O/CN, and CO, respectively (Fig. 5d). In comparison to the pure-Ch/Gel film, the fourth peak located around ∼285.1 eV reveals the incorporation of GT-CD in the Ch/Gel-CD3% (Fig. 5d). The deconvoluted N 1 s spectra (Fig. 5e,f) exhibited four characteristics components, the peak at 398.5 indicates to C-N-C, the prominent peaks at 399.2 eV assigned to -NH2 group. The other peaks for N1 s were at 399.4 and 400.6 eV, ascribed to N-H/C-N and NH3 +, respectively. The high-resolution O1s spectra (Fig. 5g,h) can be split into four individual peaks around 531.5 and 532.2 eV ascribed to N-CO/CO and -C-OH, while the peaks at 533.1 eV were assigned to C-O-C groups.

The Ch/Gel-based films' visual appearance is displayed in Fig. 6a. The Ch/Gel neat film was transparent with a yellow tint. However, when GT-CD was incorporated into the Ch/Gel polymer matrix, the composite film appeared light brown due to the tan color of the CD, and the color was uniform throughout the film, indicating that the CD was evenly distributed in the film. The yellow color became more intense as the GT-CD concentration increased from 1 to 3 wt%. Therefore, it is important to evaluate the color and optical parameters of the film. As listed in Table 1, the integration of the GT-CD significantly lowered the brightness (L-value) while significantly escalating the redness (a) and the yellowness (b) of the Ch/Gel film and the change in color values depending on the CD concentration. Consequently, the film's total color difference (ΔE) significantly increased.

Exposure to light, especially ultraviolet light, degrades food quality through photolysis and photooxidation reactions. Thus, the UV-blocking characteristics of packaging films are key film parameters that protect packaged products from photochemical reactions that cause food spoilage and extend shelf life (Mathew et al., 2019). Also, food's nutritional value and organoleptic quality (odor, color, and flavor) are greatly impacted by UV radiation (below 400 nm) (Duncan & Chang, 2012). The light transmittance at 280 and 660 nm used to evaluate the film's UV blocking property and transparency of the film is shown in Table 1. The T280 of the film significantly decreased, while the T660 slightly reduced with the addition of the GT-CD, indicating that the integration of the CD significantly enhanced UV barrier properties with a slight sacrifice to the transparency of the film.

The light transmission spectrum and UV blocking performance of the CD solutions and the Ch/Gel-based films were evaluated, and the experimental outcomes are shown in Fig. 6b-e. Fig. 6b shows the UV–vis light transmittance spectra of the aqueous dispersion of the GT-CDs (0.01, 0.02, and 0.03 wt% (w/v) in water). The light transmittance of the GT-CD dispersion was significantly decreased with increasing the GT-CD concentration. Additionally, the solution showed significantly lower light transmittance in the UV ranges, indicating the UV light absorption aptitude of the CD. The UV light-blocking ability of the solution is shown in Fig. 6c. About 74.1 ± 0.5% UV-A and 90.6 ± 0.5% UV-B light were blocked in 0.01% GT-CD aqueous solution, and the UV light was successfully blocked by increasing the concentration of GT-CD, blocking 99.1 ± 0.6% UV-A and 93.8 ± 0.6% UV-B in 0.03% GT-CD aqueous dispersion. The transmittance of the visible light of the 0.01% GT-CD aqueous dispersion was 93.9 ± 0.2% with a light yellow appearance. The transmittance lowered with the increase in the concentration; in the 0.03% GT-CD aqueous dispersion, the transmittance of visible light still reached 79.2 ± 0.2, slightly transparent with a yellowish appearance. Fig. 6d displays the UV/Vis light transmittance spectra of the Ch/Gel-based films. The neat Ch/Gel film demonstrated negligible light absorbance in the visible range and looked highly transparent, with 90.3 ± 0.7% visible light transmittance at 660 nm (T660) (Table 1). In the UV range, a small shouldering peak appeared at ∼280 nm, assigning to the π-π * transition of the CO group (carboxylic) of the Ch/Gel biopolymer blend. The visible light transmittance of the Ch/Gel film was decreased slightly by adding CD due to the light scattering and prevention of film passage by the CD particles. However, the CD-added films were highly transparent with the T660 of 83.9 ± 0.9% even in the 3 wt% of GT-CD (Ch/Gel-CD3% film) (Table 1).

On the other hand, light transmittance spectra of Ch/Gel-based films were determined, as shown in Fig. 6d. The addition of the CD reduced the light transmittance of the Ch/Gel film; however, the reduction was more significant in the UV region. The neat Ch/Gel film exhibited some UV-blocking properties due to the UV absorption capacity of the polymer itself; however, the UV-blocking properties were further increased by adding CD in the low wavelength (200–400 nm) region (Fig. 6d, Table 1). When 3 wt% of GT-CD was added, the film exhibited excellent UV-blocking properties in the UV region, blocking ∼90.2 ± 0.6% of UV-A and 99.7 ± 0.5% of UV-B (Fig. 6e, Table 1). The Ch/Gel-CD films were highly efficient in shielding UV-A and UV-B radiations without sacrificing the film's transparency (Uthirakumar et al., 2018). This UV-blocking characteristic of the Ch/Gel-CD films is expected to protect packaged products against UV-induced photo-oxidation and photo-degradation reactions.

The mechanical characteristics of the GT-CD incorporated Ch/Gel-based multifunctional films were determined by measuring tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EB) by force versus elongation plot ( Fig. 7a-d). With the loading of GT-CD, up to 3 wt%, the thickness of the Ch/Gel films was a little bit increased from 0.056 ± 0.0028–0.062 ± 0.0025 mm, depending on the concentration of GT-CD (Fig. 7a). As shown in Fig. 7b, the load bearing capacity of the Ch/Gel based films continuously increase with increasing the concentration of loaded GT-CD. The failure force of the Ch/Gel film was increased from 92.1 ± 3.2–122.5 ± 2.4 N with the integration of 3 wt% GT-CD. The TS of the Ch/Gel neat film was relatively strong and less flexible, with the TS of 65.31 ± 2.3 MPa, and the EB of 3.2 ± 0.03% (Fig. 7c,d). Adding the GT-CD to the Ch/Gel composite film produced a reinforced structure of the composite films, indicating that the incorporated CD was compatible with the Ch/Gel matrices to create a stable network formation. However, adding the 3 wt% CD decreased the EB of the Ch/Gel-CD3% film, but the decrease was not significant compared to the Ch/Gel-CD1% and Ch/Gel-CD2% (Fig. 7b). Sometimes, the addition of more than a certain level of nanofillers along with the increase in the mechanical strength increased the rigidity of the composite structure depending on the distribution of the components between the polymer matrix, which could be responsible for the slight decrease in the EB. However, the TS of the film increased to 79.54 ± 2.1 MPa (by a ∼22% increase) when 3 wt% of GT-CD was added, which was attributed to the more compact structure of the Ch/Gel-CD3% due to the uniformly dispersed GT-CD in the Ch/Gel polymer matrix and H-bonding among surface functional groups of CD and polymer matrix. A similar effect of additives (sulfur quantum dots) on the mechanical behavior has been studied in gelatin/agar-based films (Priyadarshi et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water contact angle (WCA) and water vapor permeability (WVP)
ce:section.ce:para: The surface hydrophilicity and water vapor barrier characteristics of the Ch/Gel-based films were determined through the WCA and WVP determination (Fig. 7e,f). The WCA of the neat Ch/Gel film was 62.4 ± 1.2°, verifying the hydrophilic behavior of the film (Fig. 7e). The WCA of the film was a little reduced when the GT-CD was added. The WCA of the film decreased from 62.4 ± 1.2° to 58.3 ± 1.2° when the addition of the GT-CD increased from 1 to 3 wt%, which can be due to the hydrophilic property of the CD. Adding hydrophilic materials should increase the hydrophilicity of the GT-CD-incorporated Ch/Gel-based films. But even after the addition of 3 wt% CD, the WCA is not decreasing to a significant extent. The reason could be that more CH2OH groups in Ch/Gel are attached to -OH groups of CD via intermolecular hydrogen bonds, which reduces the number of hydrophilic free hydroxyl groups in the polymer matrix, resulting in insignificant changes in the WCA.

The incorporation of the CD also influenced the WVP of the Ch/Gel-based films (Fig. 7f). The WVP of the neat Ch/Gel film was 1.44 × 10−9 ± 2.1 × 10−11 gm/m2. Pa.s and slightly increased with the integration of GT-CD; nevertheless, the variation was not statistically significant (p > 0.05). This indicated increased CD incorporation made the composite films more compact due to increased interfacial interaction between polymer matrixes. The small difference in the WVP values could be the strong interaction among the hydroxyl and carbonyl groups of the Ch/Gel polymer with the surface functional groups of the integrated GT-CD via hydrogen bonding (Xu et al., 1994). Nevertheless, the increase in WVP was not statistically significant among the GT-CD incorporated Ch/Gel films, demonstrating the inability of quantum-sized CDs to block the free space within the polymer matrices and impact the tortuosity of the path for the water molecules.

The antioxidant potential of the GT-CD and the CD-added Ch/Gel films was determined through the DPPH and ABTS methods. ABTS and DPPH free radicals assays determine antioxidative action based on the spectrophotometric changes ascending from the reaction between an antioxidant and ABTS•+ or DPPH• ( Fig. 8a). Fig. 8b displays the dose-dependent ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging activity of GT-CD compared to ascorbic acid (AA). The free radical scavenging activities of both GT-CD and AA against DPPH and ABTS increased as the concentration of GT-CD and AA increased to 100 μg/mL. The antioxidant activity of the GT-CD was comparable to or higher than that of AA. The GT-CD showed more than 90% DPPH free radicals scavenging activity and nearly 100% elimination of ABTS free radicals. The neutralization efficacy of the GT-CD can be ascribed because of the existence of oxygenated and amide functional groups on the CDs’ surface. The scavenging effect of ABTS•+ and DPPH• can be quenched or reduced through the proton transfer from -OH and amide groups of the CD to DPPH-H and ABTS-H (Khan et al., 2023c; Murru et al., 2020).

The antioxidant activity of the Ch/Gel-based films is shown in Fig. 8c. The neat Ch/Gel film showed some antioxidant activity of 33.3 ± 0.7% and 23.5 ± 0.6% for the ABTS and DPPH free radical scavenging activities, which can be attributed to the hydroxyl, amide, and carboxyl groups in the biopolymers. When the GT-CD was added to the film, the ABTS•+ and DPPH• scavenging activities significantly increased depending on the concentration of the GT-CD (Fig. 8c). The difference in ABTS•+ and DPPH• scavenging activities was due to the hydrophilic behavior of the Ch/Gel polymer films, which eases the release of GT-CD in the aqueous solution of ABTS compared to the methanolic solution of DPPH.

The release of CD from the Ch/Gel composite films was determined through food simulant solutions, and the results are shown in Fig. 8d-f. The concentration of the CD and the food simulant solutions greatly influenced the release rate of the CD. Notably, the release rate of CD increased in all simulants as the concentration of GT-CD increased. When the Ch/Gel films with the three different GT-CD concentrations were submerged in water and 10% ethanol, the GT-CD were rapidly released for the initial 1.5 h, and then, the release rate decreased, touched a steady state after 2 h in the case of the Ch/Gel-CD1% and Ch/Gel-CD2% films. However, the CD release rates of the Ch/Gel composite films in 95% and 50% ethanol solutions were significantly lower than those in water and 10% ethanol solutions. The rate of release is reduced with increasing concentration of alcohol. The polymer matrix's solubility and the solvent's affinity for GT-CD affect the release rate. Due to the hydrophilic nature of the polymer, aqueous solutions pass through the matrix easily and make the film sufficiently hydrated, resulting in a more open structure and allowing the rapid release of the CD. As a result, the CD was released more quickly and in more significant quantities in water than in alcoholic solutions (Ezati & Rhim, 2021).

The antibacterial efficacy of the GT-CD was investigated against the foodborne pathogenic bacteria Escherichia coli and Listeria monocytogenes by the agar well-diffusion method by measuring the inhibition zone and the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC)/minimum bactericidal concentration MIC/MBC evaluation ( Fig. 9a-d, Table 2). The MIC/MBC values of GT-CDs for L. monocytogenes were 160 and 200 μg/mL, while these values for E. coli were found to be 200 and 380 μg/mL, respectively (Table 2). The inhibition zone test also revealed that the GT-CDs had more antibacterial activity against L. monocytogenes than E. coli (Fig. 9b,d). Both the inhibition zone and MIC/MBC findings suggested that the GT-CD demonstrated more effective antibacterial action against L. monocytogenes than E. coli, which may be attributed to the difference in the cell wall structure of Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria (Priyadarshi et al., 2021). Listeria monocytogenes exhibited a larger zone of inhibition than E. coli. This may be because Gram-positive bacteria have one peptidoglycan layer and exposed peptide sequences in their cell wall, which allows better interaction with GT-CD and allows the CD to enter the cell interior more easily. Gram-negative bacteria, on the other hand, have thick, double-layered peptidoglycan cell walls, which complicate interactions with GT-CD (Priyadarshi et al., 2021). This result is consistent with the results obtained for various types of CD (Roy et al., 2022a).

The antibacterial activity of active packaging films is important for validating food safety and extending shelf life (Moradi et al., 2021). The antibacterial activity of the Ch/Gel-based films against E. coli and L. monocytogenes is shown in Fig. 9e,f. The neat Ch/Gel film showed bacteriostatic activity for E. coli and slight bactericidal activity for L. monocytogenes due to the antimicrobial activity of chitosan (Divsalar et al., 2018). However, the CD-added film displayed significantly strong antibacterial activity than the neat Ch/Gel film, and their antibacterial activity increased with increased concentration of CD. The 3 wt% of CD-added film completely stopped the growth of E. coli and L. monocytogenes after 3 h of incubation. It is well known that the films incorporated with CD have significant antibacterial activity (Ezati & Rhim, 2022). The high antibacterial activity of the Ch/Gel-GT-CD films may be due to the synergistic effect of chitosan and CD.

So far, studies have been conducted on the antibacterial effect and mechanism of CD's antibacterial action. The antibacterial function of CD is influenced by various variables, including electrostatic interactions, specific surface functional groups, small size and concentration, and generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). ROS generation is the most discussed method of fighting microorganisms containing singlet oxygen, hydroxyl free radicals, and superoxide anions (Travlou et al., 2018). On the other hand, CD penetrates bacterial cells and exerts its antibacterial activity. CD interacts with target molecules in the microbial cell wall and alters cell signaling, interfering with metabolic activity. An antimicrobial agent must first be absorbed directly or by electrostatic contact into the bacterial cell wall to exert antibacterial activity. When chemicals enter the cell wall, internal components leak, proteins break down, chromosomes condense, and DNA replication stops (Wang et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2016). The particle size greatly influences nanoparticle distribution in the cell membrane, and ultra-small nanoparticles can easily pass through the cell wall (Fu et al., 2022a, 2022b; Li et al., 2018). As a result, CD's quantum size can penetrate the microorganisms' cell walls. Other researchers claim that CD kills cells by impairing cellular processes. CD penetrates the cell wall and interferes with metabolic activity, proteolysis, and DNA replication, failing cellular machinery and consequent cell lysis (Wang et al., 2022).

Pork wrapped with the CD-incorporated Ch/Gel films was stored for 48 h at 20 °C and evaluated the changes in appearance, bacterial growth, and redness color (a-value) of the meat. As shown in Fig. 10a, the pork was discolored during storage due to the oxidation of myoglobin, a meat pigment, and the surface color became darker. The change in the viable bacteria count of the pork meat during storage is shown in Fig. 10b. The initial bacterial count in the packaged meat was ∼3.2 Log CFU/g but showed a rapid increase in bacterial growth during storage, especially in the neat Ch/Gel and Ch/Gel/CD1% film-wrapped samples, reaching ∼4 Log CFU/g within 24 h. However, the meat wrapped with the Ch/Gel-CD2% and Ch/Gel-CD3% films showed significantly delayed growth of bacteria. Even after 48 h, bacterial growth on the meat wrapped with the Ch/Gel-CD2% film did not exceed 2 Log CFU/g, well below the maximum acceptable microbiological limit (7 Log CFU/g) recommended by the International Commission on Microbiological Specifications for Foods (ICMSF) (Roy et al., 2022b). On the other hand, the meat packaged with the Ch/Gel-CD3% film showed less than 1 Log CFU/g of bacterial growth after 24 h and stopped the bacterial growth within 48 h. This result agrees with the antibacterial test results of the films.

Off-flavor in lipid-containing food is primarily caused by the oxidation of lipids, which produces peroxides. In order to assess the quality of the meat, the peroxide value of the packaged pork was calculated over time, and the results are presented in Fig. 10c. Depending on the kind of packaging, the peroxide value of the meat sample increased with time. The peroxide values of the neat Ch/Gel, Ch/Gel-CD1%, Ch/Gel-CD2%, and Ch/Gel-CD3% films after 24 h at 20 °C storage were ∼4.4, ∼4.1, ∼3.4, and ∼3.0 meq/kg, respectively, suggesting no significant difference (p > 0.05) between them. The peroxide value of the neat Ch/Gel and CD included Ch/Gel film samples; however, it suddenly increased after 48 h. The neat Ch/Gel sample had a peroxide value of about ∼20.2 meq/kg, while the peroxide values of the Ch/Gel-CD1%, Ch/Gel-CD2%, and Ch/Gel-CD3% samples were found to be ∼18.4, ∼15.6, and ∼13.1, respectively. Based on the concentration of GT-CD added, the value obtained for the neat-Ch/Gel wrapped sample was substantially higher (p < 0.05) than the CD-added Ch/Gel film, demonstrating the effectiveness of the functional films in avoiding lipid oxidation.

The redness (a-value) of the pork during storage is shown in Fig. 10d. The a-value of the packaged meat varied depending on the film type. The initial a-value of the packaged meat was 23.8, indicating fresh meat, but the a-value of the sample wrapped with the Ch/Gel film decreased the most, reaching 21.8 and 17.7 within 24 and 48 h of storage. In contrast, the samples wrapped with CD-incorporated films (Ch/Gel-CD1%, Ch/Gel-CD2%, and Ch/Gel-CD3%) showed much lower a-value reduction depending on the CD concentration, reaching 19.1, 19.3, 20.2 within 48 h storage. These results show the efficiency of preventing color discoloration of the CD-containing film, which is considered to be attributable to the antioxidant properties of the CD (Fig. 8b,c).

Blue-luminescent carbon dots (CDs) were prepared using green tea (GT) via a hydrothermal method and used to prepare functional Ch/Gel-based film for environmentally friendly active packaging films. The CDs were compatible with the polymer matrix to form well-blended composite films with strong antibacterial, antioxidant, and UV-barrier properties. Pork meat packaged with the CD-added functional film reduced bacterial growth and maintained meat color quality during storage at 20 °C for 48 h. The GT-CD-added functional films can be used in active packaging applications to maintain food quality, ensure food safety, and extend the shelf life of packaged foods, especially high-fat foods susceptible to photooxidation, such as chicken and seafood.

Ajahar Khan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation and analysis, Writing − original draft, Validation. Parya Ezati: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation and analysis, Writing − original draft, Validation. Jong-Whan Rhim: Conceptualization, Writing − review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
